headline: This cycle the recommended educators converge on agentic AI: how to engineer it well and why enterprises still hesitate to deploy it.
body: Cole Medin frames Google's agentic engineering playbook around the claim that the model is only 10% of success while the surrounding harness of context, tools, guardrails, and verification is 90%, positioning context engineering as the core skill and the developer's role as orchestrator rather than conductor [0]. Cyril Imhof supplies the counterweight across many shorts: agent adoption is slow because the technology is immature like early LLMs [5], building agents is harder than conventional software and demands a data-driven iterative approach [13], and security risks including prompt injection, unintended data deletion, and unauthorized actions are stalling deployment [1]. Imhof also touches infrastructure and sovereignty threads, arguing local inference can beat cloud on privacy and control [8], that building your own inference is too costly and risky [10], and that AI demand is surging high-bandwidth memory from Micron and SK Hynix [4]. Caleb Writes Code goes foundational with a visual walkthrough of GPT internals covering self-attention, multi-head attention, feed forward networks, positional embeddings, and residual connections behind models like Gemini, Opus, and Qwen [11].
